questions.question: What does the research show about group exercise versus solo exercise for mental health outcomes?

options.text: Solo exercise is always superior to group exercise
Group exercise consistently outperforms solo exercise for both adherence and mental health outcomes — social support is an independent predictor of depressive symptom reduction
There's no difference between group and solo exercise
Group exercise has no mental health benefits

questions.explanation: Research consistently shows group exercise outperforms solo exercise for both adherence and mental health outcomes. Social support during exercise is an independent predictor of depressive symptom reduction — meaning it matters ABOVE AND BEYOND the exercise dose itself. Mechanisms include behavioral activation (social commitment overrides avoidance), accountability (external structure), belonging (counters loneliness), and shared experience (normalization, reduced stigma).

questions.question: What is the difference between a 'true group' and standard exercise classes in terms of mental health benefit?

options.text: There is no difference — all group settings are equally effective
'True groups' with cohesion-building elements (team sports, clubs with social interaction, community-focused formats) are superior to standard classes across mental health measures
Standard classes are always better than true groups
Group cohesion doesn't matter for mental health

questions.explanation: Research distinguishes between 'true groups' (with cohesion-building, interdependence, shared goals, trust — like team sports, running clubs with social time, tight-knit fitness communities) and standard classes (you're in the same room but minimal interaction). True groups are superior across multiple mental health outcome categories. The social bonds and sense of belonging provide therapeutic benefit beyond just the physical activity.

questions.question: Why is social accountability particularly valuable for people with depression?

options.text: People with depression need to be punished for not exercising
Depression causes avoidance and withdrawal — social commitment creates external structure that compensates for low internal motivation (you show up for others, even when you don't feel like it)
Social accountability has no effect on exercise adherence
Only competitive athletes benefit from social accountability

questions.explanation: Depression causes avoidance, withdrawal, and low motivation — making solo exercise particularly hard to sustain. Social accountability (team sports where teammates depend on you, scheduled group classes you've paid for, running buddies who meet you) creates external structure. You show up for others, even when internal motivation is absent. This is the behavioral activation principle: action can be externally driven when internal drive is impaired.

questions.question: For someone with social anxiety who wants social connection but finds group settings intimidating, what progression does the lesson recommend?

options.text: Immediately join a competitive team sport with high social pressure
Start with low-pressure, inclusive formats (walking groups, recreational leagues, online live classes) then progress to more interactive formats as comfort builds
Social anxiety means you can never benefit from group exercise

questions.explanation: The lesson emphasizes graduated exposure for social anxiety. Start with low-pressure, non-competitive formats: walking groups (no performance pressure), recreational leagues (explicitly social, not serious), or online live classes (social connection without in-person exposure). As comfort and confidence build, explore more interactive formats. The goal is to access social benefits without triggering overwhelming anxiety.

questions.question: What's one social movement opportunity you could explore this week (local running club, drop-in yoga class, Meetup group, online live class, recreational sports league)? If you feel hesitant, what's holding you back, and is there a lower-pressure option you could try first?
questions.explanation: This reflection applies the lesson's emphasis on taking one small step toward social movement. The progression framework in the lesson suggests: Week 1 attend once and just observe, Week 2 establish routine (1-2x/week), Week 3 deepen connection (small talk, learn names), Week 4 build belonging (feel like a member). Identifying barriers (social anxiety, schedule, cost) and lower-pressure alternatives (online vs in-person, walking groups vs competitive sports) is part of realistic planning.

title: Team Sports and Social Connection - Quiz
